request: We'd like managers to approve employee leave requests, with an escalation if the manager is away.

problem.value: Avoid leave requests stalling when the manager is unavailable, ensuring timely approval/rejection.
problem.evidence: Client asks for escalation 'if manager is away' — the stated ask is a workaround for a blocking approval chain.

current_process.evidence: Platform already has absence management; unclear if single-level manager approval already exists or this is net-new.

success_metrics.evidence: No SLA or turnaround target mentioned.

actors.value: Employee (requester), Manager (approver), Escalation target (unspecified: backup manager, skip-level, HR).
actors.evidence: Explicit: manager approves. Escalation implies a second actor not named.

business_objects.value: Leave/Absence request, employee-manager hierarchy, leave balance/quota.
business_objects.evidence: Context: 'approval' almost always hides a balance/quota check.

business_rules.evidence: Core ambiguity: what defines 'manager is away' (calendar entry, OOO flag, manual delegation) and after what delay escalation fires.

workflow.value: Submitted → pending manager approval → (if away) escalated → approved/rejected.
workflow.evidence: Escalation is a state transition; trigger type (timeout vs event) not specified.

integrations.value: Likely notification to manager and escalation target.
integrations.evidence: Not stated who is notified or via which channel.

permissions.evidence: Approval circuits/permissions are shared across modules; escalation target's rights (full approve vs view-only) undefined.

config_vs_custom.evidence: Approval circuits and notification routing are client-specific per platform context; escalation logic may vary by client/country.

constraints.value: French labor law governs leave balances (paid leave/RTT); GDPR on HR data.
constraints.evidence: Context card notes regulatory constraints on absence management.

edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed: manager returns mid-escalation, no backup defined, multiple simultaneous requests, chained escalations.

reporting.evidence: No audit/compliance requirement named explicitly for this feature.

risks.evidence: Not addressed: risk of double-approval, escalation loops, or missed notifications.

questions.q: What exactly defines 'manager is away' — an out-of-office flag, an approved leave request on their own calendar, or a manually designated delegate — and after how long (or what event) does escalation trigger?
questions.why: High impact/high uncertainty: this single rule determines the entire trigger logic and whether it's timeout-based (async, needs a pending state) or event-based (manager's own leave approval).

questions.q: Who is the escalation target — a specific backup manager, the manager's own manager (skip-level), or HR/admin — and can this differ by team, department or country?
questions.why: High impact/high uncertainty: changes data model (need a delegate/hierarchy field) and whether the rule is configurable per client.

questions.q: Should escalation happen automatically after a fixed delay (e.g. 48h no response) even without an explicit 'away' signal, or only when the manager is explicitly marked unavailable?
questions.why: High impact: determines sync vs async design — a timeout-based escalation needs a scheduler/queue and a visible pending state, a signal-based one is a simple conditional reroute.

questions.q: Does a leave approval workflow already exist today (single-level manager approval), or is this the first version of approval for leave requests?
questions.why: High impact/high uncertainty: determines whether this is a new feature or a modification of shared approval-circuit logic used elsewhere in the platform.

questions.q: When escalated, does the backup approver get full approve/reject rights (including visibility of the employee's balance/history), or only a limited action?
questions.why: High impact: permissions and approval circuits are shared across modules — an oversight here typically surfaces late, at acceptance testing.

summary.objective: Ensure employee leave requests are approved without being blocked when the direct manager is unavailable, by adding an escalation path to the approval circuit.
summary.scope: Likely: extend the leave/absence approval workflow with an 'away' detection rule, an escalation state, a designated fallback approver, and related notifications — reusing the platform's existing approval-circuit and balance/quota mechanisms.
assumptions: A basic manager-approval flow for leave requests already exists and this adds escalation to it.
Escalation target is likely a skip-level manager, backup, or HR, though not specified.
The 'away' condition will be inferred from the manager's own calendar/leave status unless told otherwise.
Escalation logic may need to be configurable per client/country like other approval circuits.
summary.blind_spot: The trigger for 'manager is away' and its timing (event-based vs delay-based) is undefined — this decides whether the build is a simple conditional reroute or a queued/async mechanism with a pending state, retries and rollback handling, which is a materially different level of effort.

problem.value: Leave approval stalls when the manager is unavailable, causing delay or employee frustration; escalation is meant to unblock this.
problem.evidence: "with an escalation if the manager is away" implies the core problem is blocked/delayed approvals, not the approval mechanism itself.

current_process.value: Likely a single-level manager approval already exists (absence management is a built feature) but with no fallback when the manager is unavailable.
current_process.evidence: Context: 'Major features: absence management' already exists on the platform; request only adds escalation, suggesting base approval already works.

actors.value: Employee (requester), Manager (primary approver), an unnamed escalation approver (delegate, skip-level manager, or HR?).
actors.evidence: Request names 'manager' explicitly; escalation target is not specified.

business_objects.value: Leave/Absence request, Employee, Manager hierarchy, possibly a Delegate/Backup-approver record, and leave Balance/Quota.
business_objects.evidence: Context: existing entities Employee, Leave/Absence; approval almost always hides a balance/quota check per domain notes.

business_rules.value: Undefined: how is 'manager away' detected (explicit OOO flag/calendar, a designated backup, or a timeout with no action)? Also unclear whether quota/balance is checked before/at approval.
business_rules.evidence: Context trap: 'approval almost always hides a balance/quota check + a multi-level circuit'; request gives no detection mechanism for 'away'.

workflow.value: Likely: submitted -> pending manager approval -> (if away) escalated -> pending escalation approver -> approved/rejected. Timing of escalation trigger and whether original manager can still act post-escalation is unclear.
workflow.evidence: No states, transition timing, or race-condition handling given in the request.

integrations.value: Notifications likely needed to employee, manager, and escalation approver at each state change; channel and exact trigger events unspecified.
integrations.evidence: Context trap: 'notify' implies who/when/channel, none of which are stated.

permissions.value: Unclear who can approve after escalation, whether original manager retains visibility/veto, and who can configure escalation targets.
permissions.evidence: Context: 'permissions are forgotten until acceptance testing'; not addressed in request.

config_vs_custom.value: Likely needs to be configurable per client (or even per country, given labor-law variance) rather than a single hardcoded escalation rule.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: 'approval circuits' and 'business rules' are explicitly client-specific configurables on this platform; French labor law is a named regulatory constraint.

constraints.value: French labor law governs paid leave/RTT approval timelines; no explicit deadline or budget constraint given.
constraints.evidence: Context: 'Regulatory: French labor law (paid leave, RTT)'.

edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed: escalation target also away, concurrent multi-level escalation, employee cancels while pending, retroactive leave.

reporting.evidence: No compliance/audit requirement explicitly named yet for this feature.

questions.q: How exactly should 'the manager is away' be detected: an explicit out-of-office/calendar flag, a pre-designated backup approver, or an automatic timeout after N days with no response?
questions.why: High impact x high uncertainty: this decides whether escalation is event-driven (immediate, synchronous) or time-based (async, requires a scheduler/timeout job) - a fundamentally different build.

questions.q: Who is the escalation approver - a manually designated backup, the manager's own manager (skip-level), or HR - and can this differ by team/client/country?
questions.why: High impact x high uncertainty: determines the approval hierarchy model and whether it must be configurable per client, per the platform's client-specific approval-circuit pattern.

questions.q: Should the escalation setup (who escalates to whom, after how long) be configurable per client/team, or is a single global rule acceptable for now?
questions.why: High impact x high uncertainty: this is a configurable multi-client platform where approval circuits are named as client-specific; building it hardcoded now risks costly rework.

questions.q: Once escalated, does the original manager keep the ability to approve/reject, or is authority fully transferred to the escalation approver?
questions.why: High impact x high uncertainty: affects concurrency handling (two people racing to act) and UI/permission design for the pending state.

questions.q: How will you know this is working - e.g. reduced approval delay, fewer stuck requests - and is any audit trail of who approved/escalated required for HR compliance?
questions.why: Medium impact x high uncertainty: clarifies acceptance criteria and whether reporting/audit needs to be elevated to high-impact given HR/GDPR context.

summary.objective: Ensure employee leave requests are never stuck waiting on an absent manager, by adding an escalation path to the existing manager-approval process.
summary.scope: Extend the current leave/absence approval workflow with: (1) a mechanism to detect manager unavailability, (2) an escalation transition to a secondary approver, (3) associated notifications, and (4) permission rules for who can act at each stage. Likely touches the existing approval-circuit engine shared across modules.
assumptions: A basic manager-approval flow for leave already exists and only escalation is being added.
The escalation approver is not yet defined (backup, skip-level manager, or HR).
'Manager away' will be detected via an explicit flag/calendar or a timeout, not yet specified.
Escalation rules should be configurable per client/team given the platform's config-driven approval circuits.
French labor law and existing balance/quota checks still apply on top of the escalation logic.
summary.blind_spot: The word 'escalation' hides a trigger-detection problem (how do we know the manager is away: calendar flag vs. timeout) and a concurrency problem (can the original manager still act after escalation fires). Building the wrong trigger type (sync vs. async/timeout-based) is the single biggest cost driver here, and it's not addressed by the request at all.

problem.value: Leave requests currently stall or have no fallback when the assigned manager is unavailable, delaying employee leave processing.
problem.evidence: 'approval... with an escalation if the manager is away' implies a blocking gap in the current approval chain.

current_process.value: Manager-based approval likely already exists (absence management is a built feature); escalation path is the missing piece.
current_process.evidence: Context: 'Major features: absence management' already built — request only adds escalation.

actors.value: Employee (requester), Manager (primary approver) are explicit. Escalation approver identity (skip-level manager, HR, or designated backup) is undefined.
actors.evidence: 'managers to approve... escalation if the manager is away' names two roles but not the escalation actor.

business_objects.value: Leave/Absence request object linked to Employee and an approving Manager; likely needs an 'escalated-to' reference field.
business_objects.evidence: Context: Leave/Absence and Employee are existing core objects with approval circuits.

business_rules.value: Approval almost certainly involves an existing balance/quota check. Escalation trigger condition (how 'away' is detected) and escalation target rule are undefined.
business_rules.evidence: Context trap: 'approval almost always hides a balance/quota check + a multi-level circuit'.

workflow.value: Likely states: pending -> approved/rejected, plus a new 'escalated' state and transition; timing of escalation (immediate vs timeout) is unknown.
workflow.evidence: Request implies a new lifecycle branch but doesn't specify trigger timing.

integrations.value: Escalation and approval events likely need notifications, but recipients and channel are unspecified.
integrations.evidence: Context trap: 'notify' implies who/when/which channel — not stated in request.

permissions.value: Unclear whether the original manager retains approval rights after escalation, and what the escalation approver can see or do.
permissions.evidence: Context: 'permissions are forgotten until acceptance testing'; approval circuits are shared/cross-cutting.

config_vs_custom.evidence: Platform is configurable multi-client; approval circuits are listed as client-specific config — escalation rule scope unknown.

constraints.value: French labor law (paid leave, RTT) and GDPR on HR data apply, as with all absence handling.
constraints.evidence: Context: 'Regulatory: French labor law..., GDPR on HR data'.

edge_cases.value: Unaddressed: escalation target also away, manager returns mid-escalation, concurrent/overlapping requests, no escalation target configured.
edge_cases.evidence: Not mentioned in request; inferred from domain pattern of multi-level circuits.

reporting.evidence: HR data is GDPR-sensitive and labor-law governed; a lightweight audit of who approved/escalated is plausible even if not explicitly requested.

questions.q: How should the system detect that a manager is 'away' — their own approved leave/absence entry, an out-of-office flag, or a manual delegation they set themselves?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high — this defines the escalation trigger itself; without it, the workflow, integrations, and edge cases can't be built. Detection method changes whether it's automatic (event-driven) or manual (delegation-based).

questions.q: Who is the escalation approver — a fixed skip-level manager, HR, or a manager-configured delegate — and can this vary by department, contract, or client?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high — determines the approval circuit shape and whether it needs to be configurable per client/team, a recurring trap in this platform.

questions.q: Is escalation triggered immediately when the manager is marked away, or only after a timeout with no response — and does the original manager retain approval rights once escalated?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high — timing (event-driven vs delayed/async) and concurrent-approval rights change the state machine, notification logic, and error handling entirely.

questions.q: Should this escalation rule be a global default, or configurable per team/department/client (like other approval circuits on this platform)?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high — approval circuits are explicitly client-specific configuration in this platform; building it hardcoded vs configurable is a fundamentally different scope.

questions.q: Who needs to be notified at each step (employee, original manager, escalation approver, HR) and through which channel (email, in-app, both)?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: medium-high — 'notify' is named as a recurring trap requiring explicit who/when/channel; affects build scope for the notification layer.

summary.objective: Ensure employee leave requests are never stuck waiting on an unavailable manager, by adding an automatic escalation path to the existing manager-approval workflow.
summary.scope: Extend the existing Leave/Absence approval circuit with: (1) a way to detect a manager is away, (2) an escalation transition/state routing the request to a designated approver, (3) notifications to relevant parties, and (4) permission rules for who can act on an escalated request. Likely reuses the existing balance/quota check and multi-level circuit infrastructure already in the platform.
assumptions: Manager-based leave approval already exists; only escalation logic is new.
The escalation approver is likely a skip-level manager or HR, though not yet confirmed.
Escalation is probably triggered by the manager's own recorded absence/leave, reusing existing HR data.
The rule may need to be configurable per team, department, or client rather than a single global rule.
Standard French labor law and GDPR constraints apply as with existing absence data.
summary.blind_spot: The request assumes 'escalation if the manager is away' is a simple fallback, but it hides three undecided design choices that change the build: (1) how 'away' is detected (data-driven vs manual delegation), (2) who exactly the escalation lands on and whether that's configurable per team/client, and (3) whether the original manager can still approve after escalation fires — get this wrong and requests could be double-approved, orphaned, or stuck if the escalation target is also away.
